title: 15 Hidar — St. Stephen the Protomartyr & The Assembly
narrative:
Hidar

St. Stephen the Protomartyr (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Menas the Wonder-Worker (Abu Mena)

St. Paisios the Great, Desert Father

At the midpoint of Hidar, the monthly commemoration of St. Stephen the Protomartyr arrives with the apostolic vesting in its most complete and most costly expression. Stephen was vested twice in the Acts of the Apostles: first as a deacon, when the laying on of hands equipped him for the service of tables; then, in his trial before the Sanhedrin, as a theologian and a witness whose face shone like the face of an angel and whose speech covered the entire sweep of Israel's history with the clarity of one who had understood what the history was for. The first vesting was institutional; the second was pneumatic. The institutional vesting prepared him for the community's practical service; the pneumatic vesting prepared him for the community's ultimate testimony. In Hidar's apostolic month, Stephen at the midpoint is the reminder that the vesting the month has been distributing has both dimensions — the practical and the pneumatic — and that they belong together, that the deacon who serves tables is the same man whose face shines at his trial.

St. Menas the Wonder-Worker — Abu Mena — who appeared on the eleventh day in his capacity as the Menas of the soldier's martyrdom, returns here in his capacity as the Menas of the wonder-worker's ongoing ministry. The distinction is important: the saint is not exhausted by his historical martyrdom; he continues to act, to intercede, to heal, to distribute the grace that his sanctity makes available to the community that calls upon him. The wonder-workers are the calendar's testimony that the apostolic vesting is not terminated by death — that the commission given in life continues to be exercised from the other side of the threshold, and that the pilgrim flasks of Abu Mena's well, distributed across the ancient Mediterranean world, carried a grace that was real and that continued to produce its effects long after the man who bore it in his body had died. In Hidar's apostolic month, Menas the Wonder-Worker is the image of the commission's permanence.

St. Paisios the Great was an Egyptian desert father of the fourth century — a hermit of such extreme austerity that his story is marked throughout by miraculous provision and divine vision. He is remembered particularly for the quality of his love: a man whose interior life was so completely oriented toward God that his love for other people had the quality of the divine love — unconditional, patient, inexhaustible, extending even to those who came to his cell to test him or to harm him. In the month of the Apostolic Vesting, Paisios at the midpoint is the testimony that the vesting which Hidar distributes is ultimately a vesting in love — that the apostolic commission, however it is expressed in mission, preaching, witness, or service, is at its heart the extension of the divine love into the particular conditions of the particular community the vested person inhabits.

Stephen, Menas the Wonder-Worker, and Paisios together at the midpoint of Hidar present the apostolic vesting in its institutional, posthumous, and contemplative-loving forms. The deacon whose face shines at his trial. The martyr whose intercession continues across centuries. The hermit whose love has become the form of the divine love in miniature. All three are vested with the same commission, expressed in forms as different as human lives can be, and all three are offered to the community at the month's midpoint as the full-spectrum image of what it means to be sent. Half the month remains, and the vesting is deepening.
